coredata.dc:title: Ti–V–Mn based alloys for hydrogen compression system
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2005 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Dehouche, Z.
Savard, M.
Laurencelle, F.
Goyette, J.
coredata.dc:description: Ti–V–Mn based hydrides are one family of alloys with improved hydrogenation properties and they have a great potential to replace the AB5 alloys as the sorption materials in hydrogen compression systems, although there still are many problems associated with their use, including unstable reversible hydrogen capacity and unfavorable thermodynamic properties. To gain a better understanding on the effect of the substitution elements and to optimize the alloy composition for high storage capacity, the influence of the alloy stoichiometry was investigated. Ti–Zr–V–Mn alloys were prepared by arc melting technique and were annealed in vacuum at temperature above 900°C to obtain great sorption properties. Hydrogen absorption and desorption kinetics and PCT characteristics of these alloys at ambient temperature were measured and compared. These hydrogen storage features were also discussed in relation to the effect of alloy element compositions. Ti–Zr–V–Mn alloy cycling behavior was also examined.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Hydrogen storage materials
Gas–solid reactions
Ti–V–Mn based alloys
Cycling
Thermal compression

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: PCT comparison for LmNi4.9Sn0.1 and Ti0.9Zr0.1V0.3Mn1.28.
Desorption rate comparison for LmNi4.9Sn0.1 and Ti0.9Zr0.1V0.3Mn1.28.
Volumetric hydrogen titration system and the user Screen software.
Dynamic measurements for Ti0.9Zr0.1V0.3Mn1.3 alloy taken during activation, after 2 and after 235 cycles.
Pressure–composition isotherm of Ti0.9Zr0.1V0.3Mn1.3 at 23°C taken after the second cycle.

article.ce:title: Ti–V–Mn based alloys for hydrogen compression system

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institut de Recherche sur l’hydrogène, Université du Québec à Trois-Rivières, C.P. 500, Trois-Rivières, Qué., Canada G9A 5H7

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Ti–V–Mn based hydrides are one family of alloys with improved hydrogenation properties and they have a great potential to replace the AB5 alloys as the sorption materials in hydrogen compression systems, although there still are many problems associated with their use, including unstable reversible hydrogen capacity and unfavorable thermodynamic properties. To gain a better understanding on the effect of the substitution elements and to optimize the alloy composition for high storage capacity, the influence of the alloy stoichiometry was investigated. Ti–Zr–V–Mn alloys were prepared by arc melting technique and were annealed in vacuum at temperature above 900°C to obtain great sorption properties. Hydrogen absorption and desorption kinetics and PCT characteristics of these alloys at ambient temperature were measured and compared. These hydrogen storage features were also discussed in relation to the effect of alloy element compositions. Ti–Zr–V–Mn alloy cycling behavior was also examined.

ce:section.ce:para: Hydrogen compression is a key component in hydrogen generators and Hydrogen Research Institute (HRI) believes that metal hydride based hydrogen compression may offer advantages over mechanical compression in terms of scalability (a near-continuous range of outputs is achievable, with highest cost effectiveness in small size systems), reliability and cost.

The use of the metal hydride technology to compress hydrogen implies a judicious strategy to select reactive alloys adapted to each of the compression stages. The experimental measurements of the thermodynamic and kinetic properties of various alloys were carried out. A first selection among various alloys of the AB5 family allowing us to reach a pressure of 20atm has been carried out [1,2]. We have also started studying materials of the AB2 family and have found interesting performances in terms of speed and flow of compression [3].

The search for increased performances in term of capacity and flow of compression led us naturally towards the study of another family of materials using elements lighter than lanthanum or rare earths: AB2. This series of alloys, mainly based on the compounds containing titanium manganese (TiMn2, TiV2, TiCr2), meets these aims since they present, in solid–gas reaction, hydrogen capacities and rates of 20–50% higher than those obtained from materials derived from LaNi5 (Figs. 1 and 2
). Unfortunately, we came to realize rather quickly that their thermodynamic instability does not allow considering their use in the compressor at this moment. Our research on these alloys was therefore rather oriented towards the comprehension of the role of the various substitution components on the thermodynamics characteristics (stabilization of the plateaus, hysteresis ratios and hydrogen solubility), kinetics (improvement of the characteristics of loads and discharge) and lifespan.

The Ti-based alloys were studied extensively, because of their potential great sorption capacity at ambient temperature (∼2wt.%) with high reactivity rates [4–12]. Moreover, the position of the absorption and desorption plateaus (from 2 to 10atm) made them good candidates for the compressor project. In accordance with preceding measurements, new materials containing Ti–Zr–V–Mn were synthesized. The goal of these new measurements was to stabilize the storage capacity at 2% after the second cycle. To achieve this goal, certain materials underwent more than 200 cycles, various methods and temperatures of annealing were evaluated and various intermetallic stoichiometry (alloy composition formulations) were characterized. Following these measurements, it appeared that the properties of hydrogenation of this AB2 type were largely influenced by the quantity of Mn in the material. Manganese has a point of evaporation much lower than other metals. Thus, the synthesis by arc melting is problematic for the reproducibility of the composition of Mn in the structure of the AB2. However, by proceeding systematically, the optimal quantity of Mn could be determined. Finally, the influence of the composition variations of V and Zr were also measured.

The TiMn2-based alloy samples were prepared from pure elements (purity of Zr=99%, V=99.5% and Ti, Mn=99.9%) by arc melting under argon atmosphere with a non-consumable tungsten electrode and a water-cooled copper tray. Each ingot was melted several times in order to homogenize the alloy composition and then annealed under vacuum of 10−3 mbar in quartz tube at least 900°C for 72h in order to obtain the equilibrium BCC structure. Chemical composition of the synthesized samples was not analysed, but the sample weight difference before and after a full treatment was negligible.

Different Ti–Zr–V–Mn alloys with different Mn, V and Zr compositions are synthesized. The hydrogen sorption properties of these intermetallics were examined in order to develop the best alloy formulation having high hydrogen capacity, improved equilibrium plateau level and low hysteresis ratio.

Fig. 4 shows the sorption kinetics behaviour of Ti0.9Zr0.1V0.3Mn1.3 alloy at ambient temperature (23°C) before and after 235 cycles, and the initial absorption activation for comparison. Fig. 2 shows clearly that the degradation of the hydrogenation capacity appears only after the first cycle. Thereafter, this sample resists well to the cycling and it preserves a hydrogen capacity of 1.6wt.% H. We also observe an improvement of desorption kinetics during cycling. Hydrogen absorption and desorption isotherm taken at 23°C for activated Ti0.9Zr0.1V0.3Mn1.3 sample is presented in Fig. 5 . This result demonstrates good reversible hydrogen storage capacity. But the PCT curve shows two stepped plateau region, which reduces effective hydrogen capacity. It is also important to note that the sample show small hysteresis ratio and plateaus slope, which are helping in increasing the sorption cycle's efficiency.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of the temperature and annealing atmosphere
ce:section.ce:para: In order to homogenize the phase structure, the alloys were annealed in vacuum quartz tubes at temperatures ranging from 900 to 1100°C. We noticed that all the samples containing manganese (Mn) oxidize at temperatures higher than 950°C. In this case, the Mn vapours can react with quartz (SiO2) and thus release oxygen, which oxidizes materials. Consequently, we have determined that the optimal temperature of annealing in the vacuum quartz tubes is 900°C. A set-up for a device making it possible to heat under atmosphere of argon or hydrogen is under construction. This could allow us to obtain higher temperatures of annealing and thus, perhaps, to stabilize the thermodynamic properties of the AB2 by homogenizing more their structures.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of manganese in Ti0.9Zr01V0.3Mn x
ce:section.ce:para: The PCT isotherms of Ti0.9Zr0.1V0.3Mn x with x =1.18, 1.31 and 1.42 at 23°C show that the hysteresis and the plateau pressures are proportional to the quantity of Mn in the compound (Fig. 6 ). It turned out that the Ti0.9Zr0.1V0.3Mn1.31 alloy curve shows the high hydrogen absorbing capacity and best plateau slope. Fig. 7 shows the first sorption kinetics behaviour of Ti0.9Zr0.1V0.3Mn x alloys at 23°C. These results indicate that if we increase the quantity of Mn in the alloys starting from the above stoichiometry (Mn1.3), the equilibrium plateau pressure rises but if the quantity of Mn is reduced too much then the plateau pressure become too low and the alloy cannot desorb any more. In addition, it can be noted that increasing or decreasing amounts of manganese from the concentration of Mn1.3 has a negative effect on the absorbing capacity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of vanadium in Ti0.95Zr0.05V x Mn y
ce:section.ce:para: The PCT isotherms at 23°C of Ti0.9Zr0.1V x Mn y with x =0.15, 0.20, 0.25 and 0.30 for average Mn composition y =1.32 show that the vanadium has a strong effect on the position of the equilibrium plateaux (Fig. 8 ). The results indicate also that the more vanadium is present in the alloys, the lower is the equilibrium plateaus pressure. The vanadium element has also a positive influence on the hydrogen capacity and sorption kinetics but it does not seem necessary that its quantity exceeds x =0.2. These results are consistent with the conclusion obtained by Au et al. [11] concerning the role of vanadium in Ti–Mn alloys.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of zirconium in Ti1−x Zr x V0.3Mn y
ce:section.ce:para: The PCT isotherms at 23°C of Ti1−x Zr x V0.3Mn y with x =0.0, 0.05, 0.10 and 0.15 for average Mn composition y =1.33 show that the zirconium has also a strong influence (Fig. 9 ) on the position of the equilibrium plateaus. Indeed, this element causes a drop in the plateaus pressures but it does not affect much the hydrogen storage capacity.

Hydrogen absorbing properties of Ti-based alloys were investigated as a function of metal elements compositions. The modified multicomponent Ti–Zr–V–Mn alloys possess desirable sorption features: high hydrogen storage capacity and adjustable plateau pressure. From these results, it could be determined that the optimal stoichiometry formulation among studied Ti–Zr–V–Mn alloys is: Ti0.95Zr0.05V0.2Mn1.3. This alloy exhibits the best plateau pressures region needed for the reversible thermal compressor and superior hydrogen absorption capacity of 1.6wt.%. The Ti0.9Zr0.1V0.3Mn1.3 alloy reveals high stability during cycling and small enhancement in hydrogen discharge rate. The results also show, in comparison to the concentration of vanadium and zirconium, the manganese amount in the alloys has a strong influence on absorbing capacity. It is planned to add other elements (Cr, Nb, Mo, Cu, etc.) to check if they could have a positive influence on the sorption reversibility by modifying the phases network link. Moreover, tests in a differential scanning calorimeter (DSC) under hydrogen pressure are anticipated so that the temperatures of transition between phases can be measured. In addition, this kind of measurements could give us information on the δ-Ti phase reported by Semboshi et al. [12], which might be responsible for the degradation of the capacity after the second cycle. There are also a number of issues that need to be resolved, including the energy efficiency of the Ti–V–Mn based metal hydride hydrogen compression process, the impact of hydrogen generators gas impurities on Ti–V–Mn based metal hydride reaction kinetics and the impact that hydriding and high temperature dehydriding cycles have on Ti–V–Mn based alloy stability.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was made in partnership with Stuart Energy Systems Inc. and has been supported financially by the Ministère des Ressources Naturelles du Québec and by Natural Resources Canada.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Development of a thermal hydrogen compressor based on metal hydrides
Proceedings of the 1st Annual Report Presented to Natural Resources Canada
Development of a thermal hydrogen compressor based on metal hydrides
Proceedings of the 2nd Annual Report Presented to Natural Resources Canada
